This chapter is narrative its central narrative and its center thrust is that of prayer and connection to Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. Now often times when a person has enemies lurking, when a person has enemies working day and night to plot and plan and prod. It's very easy for a person to become overly consumed with his enemy. And so in this chapter, Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala is commanding the prophet sallall.aii wasallam stand for prayer at night most of the night is preferable half or a little less than half and recite the Quran slowly and methodically. Contemplate the Quran and doing so is going to take away from your sleep. And so you're going to have to sacrifice that sleep for this prayer. And that sacrifice will have to be compensated for during the day. Now, as Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala tells him this, he then addresses his enemies, our prophet's enemies. Why? Because it's easy for a person to say, "Well, well, I'm here busy reading and reciting and making dua and making salah. My enemies are out there getting busy. My enemies are getting ahead. But Allah subhanahu wa'ta'ala tells him, "And don't think that this is because you have forgotten your enemies. You haven't forgotten them. Neither have we. However, be patient with that which they say and boycott them in the best of ways. Because as we mentioned last night, patience is often times confused with inaction. Correct? Patience is often times confused and conflated with inaction. that if a person is being patient, they're not doing anything. No, to the contrary, patience is action. As a matter of fact, it is the default action. Before a person takes action, any action he takes, whether it be for good or whether it be for bad, whether it be for his for his friend or foe, that action needs to be based on patience, all action. And so Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala tells him, "And be patient with that which they say and boycott them, abandon them in the best of ways." What does that mean? machine. Allah says, "And boycott them in the most beautiful of ways." A beautiful boycotting, a good boycotting. If he has described this boycotting as being good, then that means there is a bad type of boycotting as well. Correct? I'll give you the what they say. I'll give you the skinny on what this bad boycotting would be. The prophet sallallah.aii wasallam retreats from their gatherings, but then he starts to spread rumors about them. When his companions come to meet him and learn about Allah, he is busy talking to them about Abu Jah and Abu Leah. And he came and he left and I saw and I think he's doing this and I think he's doing that. This is boycotting them in a bad way. He said and boycott them with poise. Boycott them with balance. Doesn't mean that the prophet sallall.aii alaihi wasallam didn't warn his companions of Abu Jah and Abu and no we've gone through many verses of the Quran in which Allah brings them up as a topic of discussion however it is for some consequence or for some benefit for some lesson to be derived not just to get that venom out it's not coming from a place of vitriol in venom. It's coming from a place of regret and we wish things could be better and we wish these men would see the light. But those whom Allah chooses not to guide will never find guidance. This is the narrative. Now, don't think that because the evildoers are able to get an occasional leg up on the good, that falsehood will occasionally will occasionally rise and cover the good. Falsehood will occasionally have a battle or two that it wins. But falsehood will never win the overall war ever. Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala assures us. He says, He says that the end result and the permanent will always be for tawa and the people of tawa. Always. Always. Even if falsehood expounds itself and it will never have the patience and longe longevity of the truth. This is momentary. This is momentary. Because if I'm directing my attention to Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala, I'm not thinking now about my enemies. Allah will take care of that for you. And this tells us what? This tells us that when we sacrifice something for Allah, Allah will replace it with that which is better. you choose to. If you choose to do so.
And so our prophet sallall.aii wasallam
is told by Allah, you now have to have
the courage to forget about your enemies
and focus on your relationship with
Allah. And if you do that, don't be
worried that they will get the upper
hand on you because you're not giving
them the attention they deserve. No, we
got you.
We will take care of that for you.
Allah assures him. He says, "Leave the
beliers to me. Leave me and the beliers
together. Leave us. Leave us be. Leave
us alone. we will take care of them for
you. He says,
he says, those men who disbelieve in
spite of the incredible amount of luxury
and comfort that they exist in.
Those men who are
just existing between,
you know, the the the the hardest thing
touching their skin is silk.
and the hardest day of their life is a
day when they are too full.
Those men, leave them to me. You focus
on your relationship with Allah. Allah
will in turn focus on your enemies for
you. And when a time comes for you to
take up arms and defend yourself, your
Lord will tell you when that time will
be. And at the end of the chapter, we'll
see that Allah gives an indicator that
that day will eventually come. A day
when the Muslim will be allowed to stand
toe-to-toe with their aggressors.
Foreham.

summary: This lecture analyzes Chapter 73 of the Quran, focusing on verses addressing the Prophet Muhammad's relationship with Allah and how to handle opposition from enemies. The speaker emphasizes that the central theme is prayer and connection to Allah, instructing the Prophet to stand in night prayer and recite the Quran methodically while being patient with those who oppose him. The lecture discusses the concept of "beautiful boycotting" - maintaining distance from enemies with dignity and poise rather than through spreading rumors or harboring bitterness. The speaker explains that patience is not passivity but rather the foundation of all wise action, and that while falsehood may occasionally gain temporary victories, truth and righteousness will ultimately prevail. The lecture also contextualizes the Prophet's courage in facing a violent, idol-worshipping society alone, emphasizing that courage and virtue are learnable traits that can be developed through trust in Allah.
suggested_title: Chapter 73 Quran: Patience, Prayer, and Dealing with Enemies - Tafsir Lecture
catchy_title: Why Prayer Matters When Facing Enemies
